title: Detecting Connected Components in a Graph: Incorrect DFS

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: The graph is represented as a list of adjacency list of edges, where each edge is a pair of node indices.
statement.outputFormat: Return the count of connected components in the graph.

constraints.description: Number of nodes in the graph
Number of edges in the graph

examples.explanation: The graph has two connected components (0, 1) and (2, 3, 4)

hints.text: Consider using a recursive DFS strategy to explore the graph.

editorial.title: Correct Approach to Detecting Connected Components
editorial.intuition: A connected component in an undirected graph is a subgraph in which any two vertices are connected to each other by paths.
editorial.bruteForce: We could perform a depth-first search on each unvisited node and count the number of connected components.
editorial.optimalSolution: We use a set to keep track of visited nodes and iterate over the list of edges to perform DFS.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: For each node that is visited during our DFS, we mark it as visited.
editorial.dryRun: Let's take the graph 0 --1-> 2 --3-> 4 as an example. We first visit node 0, mark it visited, and visit its neighbors.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case when a node has no edges
Incorrectly counting the number of connected components

functionSignature.cpp: void solve(vector<vector<int>>& edges, int n, int m, vector<bool>& visited);
functionSignature.java: void solve(int[][] edges, int n, int m, boolean[] visited);
